ground_truth.title: #33 Why You Should Read More Engineering Blogs
ground_truth.author: Shehab Abdel-Salam
ground_truth.main_content:
One of the early mistakes I made in my career was not reading engineering blogs. I used to think that the only way for an engineer to deepen their knowledge was by reading textbooks like Designing Data-Intensive Apps by Martin Kleppman, Fundamentals of Software Architecture, and so on. While these are great references to have, they often miss the real-world challenges engineers face when architecting solutions for their customers, especially at scale. A great way to learn new patterns or solutions is through stories. That's where engineering blogs can help.

Why should I read engineering blogs? The range of stories is virtually endless, with something interesting and relevant for every engineering team. A good engineering story includes Challenge/Struggle, Ideas, Solution, and Next Steps. Academic textbooks often take time to include new industry-tested ideas. So, to avoid knowledge staleness, it's important to combine work experience with modern solutions to build a strong understanding of what works and what doesn't.

Deepen your knowledge through work experience and by reading books and engineering blogs. Here are 8 blog posts where engineers share how they solved an interesting problem, including what worked and what didn't:

1. Netflix - Introducing Impressions (2025): Building a system to track every image a user hovers over on the homepage, processing from 1 to 1.5 million events per second using Apache Flink, Kafka, and Iceberg.

2. Canva - Supporting real-time mouse pointers (2024): Transitioning from a Websockets + Redis-based architecture to a WebRTC-based architecture to achieve 60 updates per second instead of 3.

3. Figma - A 9-month journey of sharding PostgreSQL for infinite scale (2024): Insights on logical vs physical sharding, with new tables experiencing only 10 seconds of partial downtime.

4. Discord - how they store trillions of messages! (2017-2023): Managing 120 million messages per day with Cassandra, later migrating to ScyllaDB to handle trillions of messages.

5. Snap - using QUIC for Snapchatters (2021): How QUIC protocol reduced network latency for live videos.

6. Shopify - Building resilient payment systems: Using ULID instead of UUID4 for idempotency keys, seeing a 50 percent decrease in INSERT statement duration.

7. Uber - Kubernetes migration journey (2025): Year-and-a-half journey migrating from Apache Mesos to Kubernetes.

8. Stripe - Building interactive docs with Markdoc: Rebuilding documentation with a custom format that simplifies the authoring experience.

Top 25 engineering blogs: Engineering at Meta, AWS Architecture Blog, LinkedIn Engineering, Discord Engineering, GitHub Engineering, Stripe Blog, Slack Engineering, Google for Developers, Uber Engineering, Spotify Engineering, Netflix Tech Blog, Canva Engineering Blog, Pinterest Engineering, The Cloudflare Blog, Medium Engineering, Yelp Engineering Blog, Airbnb Tech Blog, Shopify Engineering, Snap Engineering, Docker Engineering, Engineering at Microsoft, Dropbox Tech Blog, Auth0 Engineering, Booking.com Blog, Grafana Labs Blog.
with: One of the early mistakes I made in my career was not reading engineering blogs.
A good engineering story includes: Challenge/Struggle, Ideas, Solution, Next Steps.
Canva faced a scalability issue with supporting real-time mouse updates, which were capped at 3 updates per second.
Figma shared that new tables sharded in production experienced only 10 seconds of partial downtime.
Shopify recommends using ULID instead of UUID4 for idempotency keys as it performs better with B-tree–based indexes.
